retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Anrep Effect -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The oxide ion is very unstable, and it is rapidly converted by superoxide dismutase into hydrogen peroxide, a more stable, membrane-permeant reactive oxygen species with wide recognition as a signaling molecule. In the myocardium, a membrane-bound extracellular superoxide dismutase, a cytoplasmic copper-zinc superoxide dismutase, and a mitochondrial manganese superoxide dismutase perform the dismutation. Therefore, the oxide ion is easily converted into hydrogen peroxide at different cellular locations, upstream or downstream of the mitochondria. Sabri et al. [3] and Rothstein et al. [4] showed that hydrogen peroxide was the intracellular signal that led to the activation of kinases that phosphorylated the sodium-hydrogen antiporter I. Promoting an increased conversion of oxide ion into hydrogen peroxide leads to an enhanced slow flow response (Caldiz et al. 2007). [5] Therefore, hydrogen peroxide plays an essential role in the development of the slow flow response or Anrep effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Anrep Effect -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Stretching of the myocardium leads to mineralocorticoid activation by releasing preformed angiotensin II from the myocyte, which begins the release of aldosterone through angiotensin I receptor activation. Aldosterone activates the mineralocorticoid receptors in an autocrine/paracrine fashion. The chain of events started by the myocardial stretch is the release of angiotensin II followed by endothelin release, mineralocorticoid receptor activation, and epidermal growth factor receptor transactivation. These steps cause upstream mitochondrial reactive oxygen species release, which is responsible for activation of extracellular signal-related kinases (ERK)/90-kDa ribosomal S6 kinase (p90) signaling pathway (ERK1/2-p90) stimulating sodium-hydrogen antiporter I. [1] Once epidermal growth factor receptor transactivation takes place, aldosterone activates sodium-hydrogen antiporter I. Post-translational enhancement of sodium-hydrogen antiporter I activity results from a stretch-mediated release of preformed angiotensin II/endothelin type-I leading to activation (phosphorylation) of kinases.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Renin -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic AMP (cAMP) - cAMP is a key secondary messenger involved in cellular signal transduction in activating G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs). GPCR activation triggers the conversion of adenosine triphosphate to cAMP, which then acts on protein kinase A, which further phosphorylates essential proteins involved in protein expression, such as transcription factors. [5] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Growth Factor -- Development -- Growth Factors and Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic alterations in signal transduction pathways contribute to many cancer phenotypes such as increased cellular growth, cellular motility, and angiogenesis. One of the key pathways activated by growth factor binding (for example, EGF or insulin-like growth factor 1 [IGF-1]) to a surface receptor is the PI3K/AKT pathway. When activated by a surface receptor, phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase (PI3K) can phosphorylate and activate AKT, which is a serine/threonine-specific protein kinase. Once activated by ATK, in turn, it promotes cell survival, cell motility, angiogenesis and also inhibits apoptosis.  Apoptosis is programmed cell death. Many cancer cells show resistance to apoptosis, giving these cells a survival advantage. Many of the processes activated by AKT are known to contribute to cancer. There has, therefore, been considerable effort to develop chemotherapeutic agents that block the activation of the AKT pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Post-Transcription Modification and Drug Candidates
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphorylation plays a nominal role in gene regulation and drug response by modulating protein activity, stability, and interactions. Phosphorylation, a post-translational modification, can act as a switch to turn on or off a signal transduction pathway, potentially altering how cells respond to therapeutic agents. [63] Furthermore, kinases phosphorylate transcription factors, altering their ability to activate or repress gene transcription, which can determine the effectiveness of targeted cancer therapies. In drug resistance mechanisms, the loss of aberrant phosphorylation can reduce the efficacy of drugs. [64] Conversely, phosphorylation-based biomarkers can help predict a patient's response to kinase inhibitors, such as tyrosine kinase inhibitors used in the treatment of cystic fibrosis. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Regulation of the activity of RTKs and their downstream pathways is critical, given the vital cellular processes they modulate. Negative feedback mechanisms are varied and occur at different levels in the signaling networks. The simplest example is the direct activation of protein tyrosine phosphatases, which dephosphorylate the tyrosine residues on active RTKs. In some RTKs, like epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), downstream effector kinases PKC and MAPK phosphorylate threonine residues in the juxtamembrane region, inhibiting high-affinity ligand binding. In the MAPK/ERK pathway, MAPK phosphorylation of the upstream docking protein Gab1 and protein kinase Sos impairs their ability to interact with other proteins. In contrast, phosphorylation of upstream Raf kinase dampens its enzyme activity. All these mechanisms inhibit or terminate the cellular response to RTK activation. Alternatively, RTK activity can undergo attenuation by removing receptors from the cell surface. Active RTKs recruit Cbl, a ubiquitin ligase that tags the receptors for endocytosis and degradation via ubiquitination. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pulmonary Stress Response -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: These stress-sensing kinases phosphorylate the eukaryotic translation initiation factor and reduce global protein synthesis as a protective mechanism. It wards off stressing the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) from an overproduction of proteins, reduces the consumption of iron and amino acids in times of starvation of these proteins, and blocks viral or bacterial replication by hindering protein synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Keratitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: ATP is the primary source of phosphate. Once the protein dephosphorylates ATP and auto-phosphorylates the tyrosine residues residing in TK, the phosphotyrosines serve as docking sites for other proteins involved in signal transduction. This process is a series of complex biochemical processes that assist the cell in cell differentiation, regulation of cell growth, and cell death. The biochemical pathway is depicted in Figure 1. The crucial role tyrosine kinase proteins play in cell division has led oncologists to use the protein as a target site for targeted cancer therapy in an attempt to decrease uncontrolled cell division. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: After a mature mRNA transcript leaves the nucleus, a ribosome attaches to the transcript to begin translation. The initiation site in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes is not composed of a unique initiation sequence but is more probably a multisubstrate enzyme system based on substrate specificity. [5] [6] [7] [8] A postulated mechanism (scanning mechanism) predicts that the ribosomes initiate protein synthesis by recognizing and binding the 5′ tail of the mRNA corresponding to a 7-methylguanosine referred to as "cap." [9] The first methionine codon located in the 5′ ends of the mRNA is the initiator codon. Several factors bind to this initiation sequence (IF4F initiation factors), forming the ribosome-eIF2-GTP-Met-tRNA preinitiation complex (PIC). [10] A favorable initiation sequence in mammals is known as “Kozak consensus”: 5’ (A/G)CCAUGG 3’. The PIC scans the mRNA in the 5’ region until it reaches the AUG start codon complementary to Met-tRNA's anticodon. The first AUG codon (“leaky scanning”) can be skipped to use a downstream AUG. This creates protein isoforms when the downstream AUG is located in the same open reading frame as the first 1 or a completely different protein if it is not in-frame.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adenosine Triphosphate -- Function -- ATP in Intracellular Signaling
retrieved_docs.content: Signal transduction heavily relies on ATP. ATP can serve as a substrate for kinases, the most numerous ATP- binding protein. When a kinase phosphorylates a protein, a signaling cascade can be activated, leading to the modulation of diverse intracellular signaling pathways. [4] Kinase activity is vital to the cell and, therefore, must be tightly regulated. The presence of the magnesium ion helps regulate kinase activity. [5] Regulation is through magnesium ions existing in the cell as a complex with ATP, bound at the phosphate oxygen centers. In addition to kinase activity, ATP can function as a ubiquitous trigger of intracellular messenger release. [6] These messengers include hormones, various-enzymes, lipid mediators, neurotransmitters, nitric oxide, growth factors, and reactive oxygen species. [6] An example of ATP utilization in intracellular signaling can be observed in ATP acting as a substrate for adenylate cyclase. This process mostly occurs in G-protein coupled receptor signaling pathways. Upon binding to adenylate cyclase, ATP converts to cyclic AMP, which assists in signaling the release of calcium from intracellular stores. [7] The cAMP has other roles, including secondary messengers in hormone signaling cascades, activation of protein kinases, and regulating the function of ion channels.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Mechanism -- Activation of Cytoplasmic Kinase Domains
retrieved_docs.content: TKDs of RTKs are comprised of a C-lobe, N-lobe, and "activation loop." For a TKD to become functional, both the "activation loop" and N-lobe alpha-C helix must adopt a specific, "active" configuration. In the majority of cases, the release of cis-autoinhibition is required for this to occur. Cis-autoinhibition occurs in several ways. In Ins receptors, a tyrosine on the activation loop occupies the kinase active site, forming a stable configuration that blocks ATP and protein substrate entry.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: A series of proteins called transcription factors aid RNA polymerases in their transcription function. These factors have several functions, mainly facilitating the binding of RNA polymerase II to the promoter sequence, forming the transcription initiation complex. The initiation complex recognizes the promoter region in the DNA as a signal for the transcription starting point. The consensus sequence TATTAA (called TATA box) is located 25–35 bases upstream of the initiation site, followed by a poly-G/C consensus sequence located 32–38 bases upstream. The transcribed RNA contains multiple segments called exons and introns. Exons are segments containing the encoding sequence for protein synthesis, while introns are intervening segments of nucleotides that are excised from the primary RNA transcript (pre-mRNA) in splicing. Splice sequences are found at the 5′ and 3′ tails of introns marked with the dinucleotide GU at its 5′ end and AG at the 3′ end. Alternative splicing is a mechanism used to create different isoforms of the same protein so that several protein isoforms can be created from a limited repertoire of genes. Alternative splicing is now known to deeply influence biological behavior (sex determination, cell differentiation, and programmed cell death). [3] The mechanism underlying alternative splicing is not fully understood yet, but histone modification signatures correlate with splice site switching via chromatin-binding proteins. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The intracellular signaling pathways associated with RTKs are triggered when adaptor proteins (eg, Grb2) or protein kinases bind to phosphotyrosines on the RTK or its associated docking protein and become phosphorylated. A discussion of how ligand binding results in these phosphotyrosine-binding sites is in the Mechanism section. Phosphotyrosine binding is permitted by the Src homology 2 (SH2) and phosphotyrosine-binding (PTB) domains on the adaptor proteins and protein kinases. Adaptor proteins also contain Src homology 3 (SH3) or WW domains that target polyproline regions on protein kinases and guanine nucleotide exchange factors. These allow for the formation of signaling complexes and further protein kinase activation. RTKs utilize several signaling pathways, including:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Cellular Level -- Cell-Surface Receptors
retrieved_docs.content: Most receptors with intrinsic enzymatic activity are receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs). Ligand binding to an RTK induces dimerization and activation, leading to autophosphorylation on tyrosine residues. These phosphorylated tyrosine residues act as docking sites for intracellular signaling molecules, which in turn activate kinases and initiate signaling cascades. Examples of RTKs include insulin receptors and growth factor receptors, such as the EGF and FGF receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist, indicating that it functions by blocking hormone receptors. Atosiban is identified as a substance with a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action and is also categorized as a hormone antagonist. These relationships show that both PD 134308 and atosiban share the functional property of antagonizing hormone receptors, a characteristic that is important for therapeutic approaches aimed at modulating hormone‑mediated physiological processes.

retrieved_docs.content: antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual therapeutic roles that are essential for informed therapeutic decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist (isa), placing it within the broader category of antagonisté hormonů. Atosiban is also identified as a member of this hormone‑antagonist class (inverse_isa), meaning it functions as a specific agent that blocks hormonal activity. Both PD 134308 and Atosiban therefore share the common pharmacological property of antagonizing hormone receptors, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory enzymů function as a parent category for inhibitory syntézy proteinů, meaning that enzymes with inhibitory activity serve as upstream regulators that suppress protein synthesis. By acting on the translational machinery, these inhibitory enzymes can reduce or halt the production of new proteins, a mechanism that is central to cellular control of growth, response to stress, and the action of certain antimicrobial agents. Understanding the hierarchical relationship in which inhibitory enzymů are the primary agents that give rise to inhibitory syntézy proteinů helps clarify how protein synthesis is modulated in both normal physiology and disease states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
retrieved_docs.content: same antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are recognized for their activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد) by targeting kinases that drive leukemic cell proliferation. The epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitor group, also part of the protein kinase inhibitor and antineoplastic/immunomodulating families, includes canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, illustrating the common strategy of inhibiting receptor tyrosine kinases to suppress tumor growth. A separate therapeutic branch, hypolipidemic agents, is represented by butoxamine, diallyl trisulfide, the corticosteroid loteprednol, and dietary fiber ispaghula husk (psyllium). Although loteprednol is primarily a steroid, it is included in the hypolipidemic category, highlighting the integration of diverse compounds in lipid‑lowering strategies. Overall, the knowledge network interconnects cholesterol‑lowering drugs, hematologic therapies, BTK and

retrieved_docs.content: Atosiban is classified as a substance that exerts its effects through a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action, placing it within the broader category of hormone antagonists. This classification highlights its role in blocking specific peptide hormone receptors, which is central to its therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.content: biosynthetic pathways related to protein and muscle growth. Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1835
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté hormonů represent a broad class of hormone antagonists that includes antagonisté prostaglandinu as a subordinate subgroup. Within this hierarchy, atosiban is identified as a specific agent that belongs to the category of antagonisté hormonů. The subgroup antagonisté prostaglandinu is characterized by its functional relationship with inhibitory cyklooxygenasy, indicating that prostaglandin antagonists exert their therapeutic effect by inhibiting cyclooxygenase activity. Understanding these hierarchical and mechanistic connections aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antagonistic therapies and anticipating their biochemical actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1689
retrieved_docs.content: Atosiban is classified as a hormone antagonist, belonging to the broader group of antagonisté hormonů. It is chemically derived from vasotocin, indicating that its structure and pharmacologic activity originate from this peptide precursor. Consequently, atosiban functions within the class of hormone antagonists by modulating the actions of hormones related to vasotocin’s physiological pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2457
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist. Atosiban is likewise classified as a hormone antagonist and is a synthetic analogue that is derived from the natural peptide vasotocin. Both PD 134308 and atosiban belong to the hormone‑antagonist class, indicating that they act by blocking specific hormonal pathways, and the link between atosiban and vasotocin highlights the latter’s origin as a modified form of the endogenous hormone. This shared classification informs their pharmacological use as agents that inhibit hormone‑mediated effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1266
retrieved_docs.content: Trabektedin is a classification that encompasses both DNA minor groove binding agents and other plant alkaloids and natural products listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. As a DNA minor groove binding agent, the compound interacts specifically with the narrow groove of the DNA double helix, influencing processes such as transcription and replication. In addition, the broader group of other plant alkaloids and natural products in ATC that are members of trabektedin includes a variety of biologically active substances derived from plants, highlighting the diverse pharmacological potential within this category. Together, these entities illustrate how trabektedin serves as a unifying group for agents that target DNA structure and for a range of plant‑derived natural products used in therapeutic contexts.
